searches_performed.query: St John's wort oral contraceptive hypericum breakthrough bleeding pregnancy CYP3A4 induction
searches_performed.notes: Top: Pfrunder 2003 RCT (N=17, directly tested combination), Hall 2003 PK study, Murphy 2005, Nicolussi 2020 review.

sources.title: Interaction of St John's wort with low-dose oral contraceptive therapy: a randomized controlled trial
sources.population: 18 healthy women of childbearing age (17 completed analysis)
sources.design_notes: Crossover RCT. Low-dose OC (0.02 mg ethinylestradiol + 0.150 mg desogestrel) during: (control) OC alone, (cycle A) OC + SJW 300 mg twice daily, (cycle B) OC + SJW 300 mg three times daily. One menstrual cycle per condition.
sources.key_findings: Breakthrough bleeding incidence: control 6/17 (35%), cycle A (SJW BID) 13/17 (77%, p<0.015), cycle B (SJW TID) 15/17 (88%, p<0.001). Dose-response evident. No ovulation detected during SJW combination (progesterone remained suppressed), but follicle-like structures observed sporadically. Authors conclude SJW should be used with caution in women on OCs due to bleeding and reduced progestin levels potentially elevating pregnancy risk.
sources.limitations: Small N, single cycle per condition, healthy volunteers not real-world use. Brand of SJW: LI160 (standardized extract, 0.3% hypericin).

sources.title: St John's wort (Hypericum perforatum): drug interactions and clinical outcomes
sources.population: Review of pharmacovigilance and interaction literature
sources.key_findings: As of December 2001: 7 UK unintended pregnancies and 2 Sweden unintended pregnancies attributed to SJW + OC co-use. All nine documented pregnancy cases involved women using OCs without problems for >= 7 months before starting SJW — strengthening temporal attribution. Multiple cases of breakthrough bleeding also reported to UK and Swedish agencies.
sources.limitations: Spontaneous reports under-report true incidence; attribution is pharmacovigilance-level, not causal proof.

sources.title: Clinical relevance of St John's wort drug interactions revisited
sources.key_findings: High-hyperforin SJW induces CYP3A4 + P-gp via PXR, increasing clearance of ethinylestradiol and progestins. Low-hyperforin products (<= 1 mg hyperforin/day) showed no PK interaction in Will-Shahab et al. 2009 — suggesting product selection matters for this specific interaction. Still recommends avoiding high-hyperforin SJW with OCs.
sources.limitations: Narrative synthesis. Low-hyperforin exception rests largely on one study.

sources.title: Co-administration of St. John's wort and hormonal contraceptives: a systematic review
sources.design_notes: Recent systematic review of all available SJW-hormonal-contraceptive interaction studies.
sources.key_findings: Confirms CYP3A4/P-gp induction mechanism, confirms increased breakthrough bleeding risk, confirms pregnancy reports. Recommends alternative or backup contraception during SJW use.

claims.statement: St John's wort significantly increases breakthrough bleeding incidence in women on combined oral contraceptives, in a dose-dependent manner.
population_scope: healthy women on low-dose combined oral contraceptives

dose_threshold.a: Effect seen at SJW 300 mg BID (600 mg/day) and escalated at 300 mg TID (900 mg/day).
dose_threshold.b: Tested with 0.02 mg ethinylestradiol + 0.150 mg desogestrel. Mechanism (CYP3A4 induction) implies all combined OC regimens are affected.
dose_threshold.notes: Low-hyperforin SJW products (<=1 mg hyperforin/day) may not carry same PK effect (S3).

claims.statement: Case reports describe unintended pregnancy in women concurrently taking St John's wort and oral contraceptives, consistent with reduced contraceptive efficacy.

dose_threshold.a: Standard retail SJW doses (600-900 mg/day) implicated.
dose_threshold.notes: S2: 9 documented unintended pregnancies attributed to combination as of 2001. Under-reporting likely.

claims.statement: Mechanism is induction of CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein by hyperforin, accelerating clearance of ethinylestradiol and progestins.

dose_threshold.a: Hyperforin content matters — >1 mg/day considered high-risk; <=1 mg/day may be clinically inert (S3).
dose_threshold.b: Applies to all hormonal contraceptives metabolized by CYP3A4 (most combined OCs, some progestin-only).
dose_threshold.notes: P-gp induction additionally reduces intestinal absorption of many drugs.

claims.statement: Women taking St John's wort alongside an oral contraceptive should use a backup contraception method and watch for breakthrough bleeding.
population_scope: sexually active women on oral contraception

dose_threshold.a: Any SJW dose with measurable hyperforin.
dose_threshold.notes: Backup method should continue for at least 2 weeks after stopping SJW due to CYP3A4 induction persistence.

contradictions.topic: Low-hyperforin SJW may not trigger PK interaction
contradictions.resolution: Will-Shahab 2009 (cited in S3) reported no PK interaction with low-hyperforin SJW. This does NOT contradict the main interaction claim — it refines it. Most retail SJW products are high-hyperforin, and consumers cannot reliably verify hyperforin content on labels. Draft recommends default-avoid unless the product is clearly labeled as low-hyperforin AND the prescriber confirms.

reviewer_notes: Original INTERACTIONS entry was DANGER with 15 words and 0/2 specific sources. Severity remains DANGER: (1) unintended pregnancy is non-reversible, (2) RCT-grade evidence (S1 Pfrunder 2003) directly demonstrates the interaction, (3) pharmacovigilance documents real-world pregnancy outcomes, (4) the mechanism is well-characterized. This is stronger evidence than most DANGER pairs in the INTERACTIONS list.
